sections.heading: What Is an Aquarium Substrate Calculator and Why Do You Need One?
sections.content:
An **aquarium substrate calculator** computes how much gravel, sand, or aquasoil your tank needs from length, width, and desired depth. It converts volume into liters, weight (kg/lbs), and bag counts so you do not over- or under-buy substrate.

Substrate anchors plants, hosts beneficial bacteria, and affects water chemistry. Accurate amounts save money and support healthy fish and planted setups.

sections.heading: How Our Aquarium Substrate Calculator Works
sections.content:
Enter tank length and width, choose desired substrate depth, and select gravel, sand, or planted soil. The calculator uses:

**V = L × W × D**

Volume converts to liters (cm³ ÷ 1,000) and weight uses substrate-specific density. Results include bag count with a 10% buffer for slopes and settling.

subSections.heading: Step 1 — Measure your tank base
subSections.content: Use length and width only; tank height is not used for substrate volume.

subSections.heading: Step 2 — Decide depth
subSections.content: Use **1–2 inches** (2.5–5 cm) for fish-only tanks or **2–4 inches** (5–10 cm) average for planted tanks. Average high and low points for sloped aquascapes.

subSections.heading: Step 3 — Pick substrate type
subSections.content: Gravel (~1.6 kg/L), sand (~1.4 kg/L), aquasoil (~0.9 kg/L), ADA Amazonia (~0.85 kg/L), Fluval Stratum (~0.75 kg/L), or Seachem Flourite (~1.3 kg/L).

sections.heading: Understanding Different Types of Aquarium Substrates

subSections.heading: Aquarium Gravel
subSections.content: Durable 2–5 mm gravel for fish-only or lightly planted tanks. Medium density (~1,000 cm³ per kg). Rinse thoroughly before use.

subSections.heading: Aquarium Sand
subSections.content: Fine 0.5–2 mm sand for natural looks. Packs denser than gravel; add ~10% extra volume. Ideal depth **1–2 inches** for fish-only setups.

subSections.heading: Aquasoil and Planted Substrates
subSections.content: Nutrient-rich soils (ADA Amazonia, Fluval Stratum, Seachem Flourite) for planted tanks. Lighter density; plan **3–5 inches** average for heavy root feeders. Cap with sand or gravel to reduce clouding.

sections.heading: Recommended Substrate Depth
sections.content: Fish-only: **1–2 inches**. Planted: **2–4 inches** average with slopes (shallow front, deeper back). Carpeting plants often need **3+ inches** minimum.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes to Avoid
sections.content:
- Using tank height instead of substrate depth
- Ignoring slope averaging
- Skipping the 10–15% buffer
- Forgetting substrate density differs by type
- Not rinsing substrate before adding it to the tank

sections.heading: Pro Tips
sections.content:
- Rinse all substrates until water runs clear
- Build gentle slopes for planted displays
- Test water after adding new aquasoil
- Vacuum only the top layer during maintenance
- Track bag sizes for your preferred brand in the calculator

faq.question: How much substrate for aquarium do I need as a general rule?
faq.answer: About **1–2 pounds per gallon** for gravel at 1-inch depth, but always use the calculator for your exact tank size and depth.

faq.question: What is the best depth for gravel?
faq.answer: **1–2 inches** for fish-only tanks and **2–4 inches** average for planted setups.

faq.question: Does the sand calculator differ from gravel?
faq.answer: Yes. Sand packs denser, so weight is higher for the same volume. The calculator adjusts by substrate type.

faq.question: Can I use the planted substrate calculator for fish-only tanks?
faq.answer: Yes. Choose lower depth and inert gravel or sand for fish-only setups.

faq.question: How do I handle sloped substrate?
faq.answer: Enter the **average** of your highest and lowest depth points. Add 10–15% buffer for settling.

faq.question: How often should I recalculate substrate needs?
faq.answer: Only when rescaping or upgrading tanks. Regular maintenance preserves your original layer.
